coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic activity analysis of vinegar residue-based heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots integrated on vertically aligned graphene arrays for hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Bin
Tian, Yuchen
An, Yang
Liu, Rong
Shaik, Firdoz
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based materials are considered cost-effective electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Here we design the heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) loaded on vertical graphene (VG) as HER catalyst by density functional theory (DFT). And heteroatoms (B/N/P/S)-doped CQDs is synthesized with vinegar residue as the carbon source, which are integrated on VG as HER electrocatalysts. The results of electrochemical measurements are consistent with the DFT calculations. The heteroatom of CQDs in heteroatom-doped CQDs loaded on VG (CQDs-VG) is the leading active site in the HER process, which increases the catalytic potential of VG. The VG substrate increases the electron density of the heteroatom, which decreases the energy barrier and improves the catalytic activity. The N-doped catalyst shows the best potential in the HER process. This work contributes for the development of carbon based cost-effective electrocatalysts for water-splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Vinegar residue
Heteroatom doping
Carbon quantum dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of heteroatom-CQDs-VG@Cu, CQDs-VG@Cu, and VG@Cu
Hydrogen adsorbed on different sites of CQDs-VG and different sites of H-CQDs-VG with same doping from
Heteroatom(B/N/P/S)–2C doped in CQDs-VG and CQDs with six-membered hetero compounds
Heteroatom(B/N/P/S)–2C doped in CQDs-VG and CQDs with five-membered hetero compounds
XRD, and XPS analysis of H-CQDs-VG@Cu after HER

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Gibbs free energy of different sites on CQDs-VG (a). The Gibbs free energy with same heteroatom doping from of different sites on H-CQDs-VG (b).
The Gibbs free energy H-CQDs-VG and H-CQDs (a). The p band of heteroatom of H-CQDs-VG and H-CQDs (b–e). The p band center analysis of H-CQDs-VG.

Charge density difference analysis of H-CQDs-VG, B-CQDs-VG (a), N-CQDs-VG (c), P-CQDs-VG (e), and S-CQDs-VG (f). 2D section view of charge density difference of heteroatom-hydrogen bond, B–H (b), N–H (d), P–H (f), S–H (h).

The Gibbs free energy of H-FHC-CQDs-VG and H-FHC-CQDs (a). PDOS of heteroatom in H-FHC-CQDs-VG and H-FHC-CQDs (b–c). The p-band center of H-FHC-CQDs-VG (d). The red dashed line represents the p-band center. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The Gibbs free energy of H–3C (a). The PDOS of the p-band of H–3C (b), The black dashed line represents the Ef, the red dashed line represents the p-band center. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a–d) The XPS analysis of H-CQDs-VG@Cu. The atomic content analysis of H-CQDs-VG@Cu

The electrochemical measurement results of H-CQDs-VG@Cu in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4. LSV curves (a) and Tafel slope (b) of different concentrations of N-CQDs loaded on graphene. HER chronopotentiometry curves (c) for different concentrations of N-CQDs loaded on graphene recorded at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2.

The electrochemical measurement results of H-CQDs-VG@Cu in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4. LSV curves (a) and Tafel slope (b) of H-CQDs-VG and Pt/C@Cu. HER chronopotentiometry curves (c) for H-CQDs-VG@Cu recorded at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2. (d)The Nyquist plots of H-CQDs-VG@Cu, the insets are the equivalent circuit used to fit the impedance spectra and the ohmic resistances of H-CQDs-VG@Cu.
(a) The XRD analysis of N-CQDs-VG@Cu. The XPS analysis of N-CQDs-VG@Cu, C 1s (b), N 1s (c), N content analysis (d).

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic activity analysis of vinegar residue-based heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots integrated on vertically aligned graphene arrays for hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Urban and Environmental Science, and Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Earth Surface System and Environmental Carrying Capacity, Northwest University, Xi'an, Shaanxi 710127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Urban and Environmental Science
and Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Earth Surface System and Environmental Carrying Capacity

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Urban and Environmental Science, and Shaanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Earth Surface System and Environmental Carrying Capacity, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710127, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Vignan's Foundation for Science, Technology and Research, Vadlamudi, Guntur 522213, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, Vignan’s Foundation for Science, Technology and Research, Vadlamudi, Guntur 522213, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based materials are considered cost-effective electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Here we design the heteroatom-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) loaded on vertical graphene (VG) as HER catalyst by density functional theory (DFT). And heteroatoms (B/N/P/S)-doped CQDs is synthesized with vinegar residue as the carbon source, which are integrated on VG as HER electrocatalysts. The results of electrochemical measurements are consistent with the DFT calculations. The heteroatom of CQDs in heteroatom-doped CQDs loaded on VG (CQDs-VG) is the leading active site in the HER process, which increases the catalytic potential of VG. The VG substrate increases the electron density of the heteroatom, which decreases the energy barrier and improves the catalytic activity. The N-doped catalyst shows the best potential in the HER process. This work contributes for the development of carbon based cost-effective electrocatalysts for water-splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: With the growth of social energy demand, the accelerated consumption of fossil fuels leads to enhanced global warming problems which in turn shows adverse changes with climate and environment [1,2]. Hydrogen is considered sustainable and pollution-free energy substituted for fossil fuels [3]. Water splitting is considered one of the most effective green technologies for the mass production of hydrogen [4,5], which mainly consists of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). As an essential process to obtain hydrogen energy, the HER process attracts much attention from researchers [6,7]. However, the high energy barrier input for HER process limits the usage of water splitting for commercial applications [8]. To improve the efficiency of HER process, cost-effective electrocatalysts are greatly needed [9,10]. The activity of carbon-based electrocatalysts is primarily depends on the doping elements, and the productivity of the HER process is significantly influenced by the various forms of doping elements utilized [11]. Computational chemistry becomes a primary driving force for the development of efficient electrocatalysts [12,13].

For several decades, platinum considered as an efficient electrocatalyst for HER process [14]. However, the high price and scarcity of platinum hinder the development of the water-splitting technology for commercial applications [15], and there is a great need to design and develop cost effective electrocatalysts with enhanced efficiency. Carbon based materials act as potential candidates and substitutes with metal based materials owing to low cost, less pollution and ecofriendly in nature [16]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered as potential electrocatalysts for HER process owing to zero-dimensional size and increased atomic defects which may improve its electrocatalytic activity in recently years [17,18]. However, pure CQDs are prone to agglomeration [19]. To avoid the agglomeration of CQDs, graphene has been used as the substrate for CQDs in recent years due to their good synergy with CQDs in the catalytic process [20]. Liao et al. reported a facile one-pot hydrothermal method for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/carbon nanotubes [21], to improve the catalytic activity of N-doped carbon nanotubes (CNTs) in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process. They systematically evaluated the electrocatalytic activity of N-doped CQDs/CNTs during the ORR reaction. They investigated that the synergistic interaction between N-doped CQDs and carbon-based substrates plays an essential role in enhancing the catalytic activity. Liu et al. [22] report a three-dimensional nano catalyst for the ORR reaction, consist of multi-wall CNTs integrated with doped CQDs using B and N heteroatoms. CQDs/CNTs exhibit enhanced electrocatalytic activity compared to CNTs. CQDs improve the catalytic activity of the carbon-based substrate. The synergistic effect of CQDs with carbon-based substrates is the main reason for enhancing the catalytic performance of electrocatalysts. But two-dimensional graphene aggregates tend to form during fabrication process, thereby decreasing the specific surface area resulting in inferior ion transport capabilities. Vertical graphene (VG) can improve the ion transport capabilities and accelerate the electrocatalytic rate due to its high specific surface area and high porosity [23]. Our previous study confirms that CQDs loaded on VG (CQDs-VG) materials can improve the catalytic activity and electrochemical performance in the HER process [24], and the nitrogen-doping of CQDs-VG can further enhance the catalytic performance. Nevertheless, the effect of heteroatoms dopants on the catalytic activity enhancement of CQDs-VG and the mechanisms related to different activities are still unexplored.

In this study, a theoretical model for the heteroatom-doped CQDs-VG electrocatalysts is developed and the catalytic activity of heteroatom-doped CQDs-VG electrocatalysts is calculated and analyzed by the density functional theory (DFT). And the results of DFT calculations are further verified by electrochemical measurement results. The synergistic effect between CQDs and VG is unraveled. The effect of different forms of doping between different heteroatoms on the HER process is explored. This work will be useful for the rational design and development of cost-effective carbon based electrocatalysts with enhanced catalytic activity for HER.

The heteroatom-doped active sites and the activity of various CQDs-VG for HER performance were calculated using DFT with spin polarization using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) [25]. The electron exchange correlation function was described using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof function [26], and the interactions between the nuclei and electrons of atoms were defined using the projector augmented wave [27]. The wave function of the electron expanded on a plane wave basis with a cutoff energy of 500 eV. All calculations were set the convergence tolerance for energy and force to 10−5 eV and 10−4 eV/Å. The K-points were set to be 3 × 3 × 1 for all models.

For all calculational models, the hydrogen adsorption energy on CQDs-VG surfaces (ΔE ads) were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Δ
E
a d s
= E
(
s u b s t r a t e + H ∗
)
−
E
(
s u b s t r a t e
)
−
1 2
E
H 2
where E (substrate + H∗) , E (substrate), and
E
H 2
represented the total energy of the substrate-hydrogen system, the energy of the substrate and the energy of H2 molecule in the gas phase, respectively. The catalytic activity is measured by calculating the ΔG H∗ value [28]. The smaller the |ΔG H∗| value, the higher the catalytic activity of the adsorption site [29]. The Gibbs free energy of hydrogen adsorption (ΔG H∗) was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
Δ G
H ∗
=
Δ E
a d s
+ Δ E
Z P E
–
T Δ
S H
where ΔE ZPE represented the difference of zero-point energy of hydrogen between the adsorbed and gas-phase. ΔS indicated the changes of entropy between the adsorbed and gaseous, and T was temperature. TΔSH was calculated as 0.20 eV in Ref. [30]. And the detailed computational information of the free-energy calculations is shown in Table S1.

The basic model of graphene single cell derived from Materials Studio. VG and CQDs formed a double-layer carbon atom structure. CQDs were added to the center of VG's upper layer in the original model. The second layer of the CQDs-VG model was frozen in calculations to simulate VG part. And the first layer of the CQDs-VG model was comprised of 2 × 2 unit cells, which was free in the calculation. The 15 Å vacuum layer was added in the z-axis direction to all the computational models to avoid the influence of periodic structures. The results of structural relaxation were shown in Fig. S1.

As shown in Fig. S2, three representative hydrogen atom adsorption sites are selected to explore the optimal adsorption sites for the hydrogen atom, which are hydrogen atom adsorbed above the CQDs, hydrogen atom adsorbed under the CQDs, and hydrogen atom adsorbed on the inclined plane between the VG and CQDs. According to previous records [31,32], heteroatoms were mainly doped in carbon with two different forms. Heteroatom formed chemical bonds with two surrounding carbon atoms (H–2C), and heteroatom formed chemical bonds with three surrounding carbon atoms (H–3C). In H–2C, N-doped and S-doped were present in the form of doping of five-membered heterocyclic compounds (H-FHC) and six-membered heterocyclic compounds. On this basis, a series of atomic models of heteroatom doping on CQDs-VG were constructed to provide a comprehensive discussion and study of H-CQDs-VG electrocatalysts. As shown in Fig. S3, we built heteroatom doping models in VG (H–3C-VG-CQDs) and CQDs (H–3C-CQDs-VG) to investigate the improvement of CQDs for VG. The models for heteroatom(B/N/P/S)-doped in CQDs (H-CQDs) and H-CQDs-VG were shown in Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. S5, the models of the H-FHC doped in CQDs-VG (H-FHC-CQDs-VG) and CQDs (H-FHC-CQDs) were constructed. And the models of H–3C doping in CQDs-VG were shown in Fig. S6.

HCl (37.5%), H2SO4 (98%), H2O2 (30%), KOH, NaNO3, KMnO4, Na2SO4, boric acid, urea, phytic acid, 20% Pt/C, isopropanol, and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nafion (5 wt%) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Polytetrafluoroethylene was purchased from Yangzhong Hongda Fluoroplastics Co., Ltd, which height was 30 mm, the inner diameter was 44 mm, and the thickness was 2 mm. Copper sheets were purchased from Zhuji Yishun Building Materials Co., Ltd. All experiments used deionized water and All reagents were analytical grade.

Graphene was prepared by a modified hummers method [33]. 1 g NaNO3 and 1 g graphite powder were added to 46 mL concentrated sulfuric acid under ice-water bath conditions and stirred for 2 h. Then 8 g KMnO4 was added to the mixture during the stirring process. The solution was transferred to an oil bath and stirred for 3 h. 100 mL of deionized water and 12 mL H2O2 were added to the resulting solution and stirred for 10 min, and then the solution was added 100 mL of deionized water. The solution was washed with hydrochloric acid and deionized water to pH = 6. The product was placed in an oven and dried at 80 °C for 4 h. 0.12g graphene was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and 300 μL ethanol. The solution sonication for 1 h followed by directional freeze-drying for 24 h to obtain VG.

The method of carbon source precursors of CQDs as in our previous work [24], the vinegar residue was kept in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 6 h to obtain biocarbon. The most favorable heteroatom doping concentration for the electrocatalytic reaction could be obtained during the hydrothermal reaction temperature of 200 °C [34]. The method of synthesis B-CQDs was improved from Ref [35]. 0.1 g product and 1 g boric acid were dissolved in 33 mL deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1 h. Next, the mixed solution was transferred to the reactor at 200 °C for 10 h. After that, it was filtered twice, and the preparation of the B-CQDs solution was completed. The method of synthesis N-CQDs and P-CQDs was improved from our previous work [24,36]. 0.1 g of product and 0.3 g of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1 h. Then the mixture was transferred to the reaction kettle at 200 °C hydrothermal heats for 12 h. The mixed solution was filtered twice to obtain the N-CQDs solution. 0.1 g product and 0.03 g phytic acid solution were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1 h, then the mixed solution was transferred to a reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the hybrid solution was filtered twice to obtain the P-CQDs solution. The method of synthesis S-CQDs was improved from Ref [37]. 0.1 g product and 0.2 g sodium sulfate were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1 h. The mixed solution was transferred to a reactor and kept at 200 °C for 8 h. After that, the solution was filtered twice to obtain the S-CQDs solution. 0.1 g product was dissolved in 40 mL deionized water and ultrasonicated for 1 h. The mixed solution was transferred to a reactor and kept at 200 °C for 8 h. Then, the solution was filtered twice to obtain the CQDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of heteroatom-CQDs-VG@Cu, CQDs-VG@Cu, and VG@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mL, 10 mL, 15 mL, 20 mL, and 25 mL of the prepared N-CQDs solution were added 5 mL deionized water, to which were added 0.12 g, 0.18 g, 0.24 g, 0.3 g, and 0.36 g VG to get the N-CQDs-VG solution of different N-CQDs concentrations. 5 mL solution of the prepared H-CQDs, and CQDs were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water with 0.12 g VG. The above mixture solutions were ultrasonicated for 4 h to get the solution of heteroatom-CQDs-VG (H-CQDs-VG), and CQDs-VG. 0.12 g VG was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water to obtain the VG solution. The solution of H-CQDs-VG, CQDs-VG, and VG were dropped on the surface of the copper sheet (about 1 cm−2 in size) that had been soaked and washed in ethanol and deionized water, then the Nafion solution was dropped in the mixed solution. Finally, the mixture was transferred to an oven at 60 °C for 1 h to obtain the H-CQDs-VG@Cu, CQDs-VG@Cu, and VG@Cu.

5 mg of 20%Pt/C, 90 μL deionized water, 900 μL of isopropanol, and 10 μL of Nafion was treated by ultrasound under an ice bath for 15 min to obtain Pt/C ink. The Pt/C ink was dropped on to surface of copper sheet, and transferred to an oven at 60 °C for 30 min to obtain the Pt/C@Cu.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on Cu Kα radiation in Bragg-Brentano (θ-2θ) configuration. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+) were performed for the characterization of the materials.

Electrochemical performance measurements were performed using CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd.). In the electrochemical test of the three-electrode system, mercury intrusion sulfuric acid electrode and graphite electrode were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The electrocatalytic performance of HER in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 electrolytes was investigated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scan speed of 5 mVs−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen adsorbed on different sites of CQDs-VG and different sites of H-CQDs-VG with same doping from
ce:section.ce:para: The |ΔG H∗| of different active sites is shown in Fig. 1 a, the lowest |ΔG H∗| value of the hydrogen above CQDs is 1.6623 eV, which is the best adsorption site for hydrogen atom adsorption on CQDs-VG. And the |ΔG H∗| value of hydrogen adsorbed on the inclined plane between the VG and CQDs is −2.1395 eV, which is the worst catalytic activity site. The electrocatalyst has the best catalytic performance when a hydrogen atom is adsorbed on top of CQDs. Hydrogen atoms are difficult to adsorb and desorption on the saturated as well as even on the surface formed by the electrons between CQDs and VG [38], which the plausible reason for the best catalytic activity of hydrogen atoms attach on the top of CQDs. The hydrogen atom is added on top of active sites in the subsequent calculation.

As shown in Fig. 1b, the solid line indicates ΔG H∗ of H–3C-CQDs-VG, and the dashed line indicates ΔG H∗ of H–3C-VG-CQDs. It is evident that heteroatom doping in CQDs has a lower |ΔG H∗| value than in heteroatom doping in VG. The N-doped shows the best catalytic activity with a |ΔG H∗| value of 2.6670 eV for N–3C-CQDs-VG, which is a significant decrease in energy barrier compared to N–3C-VG-CQDs (3.1239 eV). The energy barrier decreases from 1.0028 eV to 0.9228 eV for B–3C-VG-CQDs compared to B–3C-CQDs-VG. The same result is reflected in P-doped and S-doped. For P–3C-VG-CQDs compared to P–3C-CQDs-VG, the energy barrier decreases from 0.3805 eV to 0.3276 eV. And the energy barrier decreases from 2.0074 eV to 1.6895 eV for S–3C-VG-CQDs compared to S–3C-CQDs-VG. H–3C-CQDs-VG is more favorable for hydrogen atom adsorption due to its lower energy barrier. It indicates that the heteroatom of H–3C-CQDs-VG is the leading catalytic active site in the HER process. Heteroatom of CQDs plays the role in the main catalytic active site of H-CQDs-VG electrocatalysts in the HER process, which provides lower energy barrier for HER process of carbon-based catalytic and increases the catalytic potential of VG. We focus our subsequent calculations on the catalytically active site of the heteroatom doped in CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heteroatom(B/N/P/S)–2C doped in CQDs-VG and CQDs with six-membered hetero compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The ΔG H∗ is shown in Fig. 2 a. In the same doping form, the |ΔG H∗| value of H-CQDs-VG is generally lower than the |ΔGH∗| value of H-CQDs. The |ΔG H∗| value of N-doped is increased from 0.6899 eV to 0.2066 eV by adding VG substrate, which is the most significant improvement of H-CQDs-VG. The |ΔG H∗| value of B-doped decreases from 0.7330 eV to 0.4312 eV. A similar result is shown in P-doped and S-doped, the energy barrier is reduced by 0.0307 eV and 0.0333 eV, respectively. H-CQDs-VG has a lower |ΔG H∗| value compared to H-CQDs, which proves that the catalytic performance of heteroatoms doping CQDs is enhanced by adding VG substrate in the design and preparation of electrocatalysts [39]. The Projected Density of State (PDOS) analysis is shown in Fig. 2b–e. The black dashed line represents the Fermi energy level (Ef), which is the highest energy level occupied by electrons when the temperature is absolute zero. It is evident that the electron density of heteroatom in H-CQDs-VG is higher than that of the same heteroatom in H-CQDs at Ef, which is indicating the heteroatom on the active sites in H-CQDs-VG can provide more electron density than the active sites in H-CQDs during HER process and provide a stronger ability to attract hydrogen atoms for improved catalytic activity [40]. N-CQDs-VG has the lowest |ΔG H∗| value (0.2066 eV) in the different heteroatoms and shows the best catalytic activity in the HER process. The other catalytic activities are B-CQDs-VG (0.4312 eV), P-CQDs-VG (1.1259 eV), and S-CQDs-VG (1.9208 eV) in descending order. According to molecular orbital theory [41,42], the hydrogen atom is adsorbed on the catalyst surface, the hybridization energy bands in which the outermost electron energy band of the catalyst interacts with the electron energy band of the hydrogen atoms can be divided into two groups, antibonding orbital, which generally crosses Ef, and bonding orbital, which energy level is lower than Ef [43,44]. The strength of the adsorption of the catalyst is correlated with the anti-bonding orbitals, the higher the outermost electron energy band center of H-CQDs-VG, the higher the average energy level of the bonding and anti-bonding orbitals, the higher the anti-bonding orbitals, the lower the electron occupancy, and the stronger the interaction between the catalyst and the hydrogen atom [45–47]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the higher the energy level of the p-band center of H-CQDs-VG, the higher the catalyst activity. The p-band center energy level of N-CQDs-VG is −3.9538 eV, and the p-band center energy level of S-CQDs-VG is −4.2457 eV. The p-band center energy level of B-CQDs-VG and P-CQDs-VG is −4.1839 eV and −4.2050 eV. It is obvious that N-CQDs-VG has stronger adsorption between N-CQDs-VG and H atom, which further explains the reason for N-CQDs-VG having the best catalytic activity in the HER process.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the light blue area represents the area that lost electrons, and the yellow area represents the area that gained electrons. In the transition state, the blue area is distributed around the heteroatom, and the yellow area is distributed around the hydrogen atom. It is indicated that electrons are transferred from heteroatom to hydrogen atom to form chemical bonds [15,48]. As shown in Fig. 3a, the B atom loses electrons, and the H atom gains electrons. As the electronegativity and extra-nuclear electrons of the heteroatom increase, a significant region of lost electrons outside the region of increased electron density around the hydrogen atom increase gradually in N-doped (Fig. 3c), P-doped (Fig. 3e) and S-doped (Fig. 3g). The region between the heteroatom and the hydrogen atom to gain electrons also gradually increases, which indicates that the formation of heteroatom-hydrogen bonds is more stable, and the hydrogen atom is more difficult to desorb [12,49,50]. The electron density acquired by the hydrogen atom is also an essential indicator of the catalytic activity of the HER process [51]. In the 2D cross-sectional view of the heteroatom-hydrogen bond charge density difference, the red regions represent the regions in the overall structure that have gained the most electrons, and the blue areas represent the regions that have lost the most electrons [52]. The hydrogen atom in N-CQDs-VG increases the most electron density and gains the most electrons with 0.1338 e (Fig. 3d). The hydrogen atom in B-CQDs-VG obtains 0.1304 e electrons (Fig. 3b), which is less than N-CQDs-VG. In other models of heteroatom-doped CQDs-VG, the number of electrons obtained by hydrogen atoms is P-CQDs-VG (Fig. 3f) and S-CQDs-VG (Fig. 3h) in descending order. When hydrogen atoms are adsorbed on H-CQDs-VG to form a chemical bond, the more electrons are transferred from the heteroatom to the hydrogen atom, the stronger the interaction between the heteroatom and the hydrogen atom, and the more stable the chemical bond formed [40,53]. The catalytic activity of H-CQDs-VG is accompanied by an increase in the amount of electron transfer received by the hydrogen atom adsorbed on H-CQDs-VG [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heteroatom(B/N/P/S)–2C doped in CQDs-VG and CQDs with five-membered hetero compounds
ce:section.ce:para: The energy barrier of H-FHC in the HER process is shown in Fig. 4 a, the |ΔG H∗| in the HER process of N–FHC–CQDs-VG is −0.7236 eV, which is lower than the |ΔG H∗| in the HER process of N–FHC–CQDs (−0.9177 eV). S–FHC–CQDs-VG have better performance (0.2281 eV) in the catalytic activity of the HER process compared to S–FHC–CQDs (0.3160 eV). Among the same elements doped in CQDs-VG and CQDs, the addition of VG substrate makes H-FHC exhibit better catalytic activity. And the reason for such a result can be seen in the energy band analysis of the doped atoms. As shown in Fig. 4b, it is evident that the electron density of N in N–FHC–CQDs-VG is higher than the N in N–FHC–CQDs at Ef, and H-FHC-CQDs-VG in S-doped exhibits a higher electron density at the Ef. The stronger ability to attract H atom in the hydrogen adsorbed on H-FHC-CQDs-VG system in the HER process. The VG substrate effectively increases the electron density of the heteroatom at the Ef and increases the catalytic activity of the catalyst in the HER process. As shown in Fig. 4d, the p-band center of S–FHC–CQDs-VG (−4.2256 eV) is obviously higher than N–FHC–CQDs-VG (−4.2388 eV), indicating that the antibonding orbitals of S–FHC–CQDs-VG has a higher antibonding orbital, heteroatom bonds to H atom with more electrons in bonding orbitals and stronger interactions with H atom than N–FHC–CQDs-VG. That further explains why S-FHC doped is the best catalytic activity in this doped form.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, P–3C has the lowest |ΔG H∗| value is 0.3105 eV. N–3C has the highest |ΔG H∗| value is 2.0176 eV. The catalytic activity of S–3C (1.5641 eV) is slightly better than that of N–3C. And the catalytic activity of B–3C (0.8900 eV) is second only to that of P–3C. P–3C is the optimal catalytic in the HER process for H–3C doping forms. The p-band center analysis is shown in Fig. 5b, P–3C (−4.1015 eV) has the highest p-band center. N–3C (−4.2942 eV) has the lowest energy level of the p-band center. The p-band centers are −4.1048 eV and −4.1283 eV in B–3C and S–3C. It is indicated that P–3C has the strongest interaction with the H atom in H–3C, which further proves that P–3C is the best electrocatalyst in H–3C.

The result of XPS analysis is shown in Fig. 6 a–d. The full spectrum of H-CQDs-G@Cu shows that the heteroatoms are successfully doped in the electrocatalyst. And the element content is shown in Fig. 6e, the content of heteroatoms in B-CQDs-G@Cu, N-CQDs-G@Cu, P-CQDs-G@Cu, and S-CQDs-G@Cu is 4.02%, 3.73%, 3.60% and 3.72% respectively. The heteroatom doping content has similar results to the optimal doping content report in references [35,55–57].

The catalytic activity analysis of the electrocatalysts toward the HER process in 0.5 M H2SO4 is shown in Fig. 7 a. The effect of different concentrations of CQDs loaded on graphene is compared. 5-N-CQDs-VG@Cu shows the lowest overpotential in the HER process with an overpotential of only 496 mV in 10 mA cm−2. With the increase of CQDs concentration, the overpotential of the electrocatalyst at 10 mA cm−2 increases. 10-N-CQDs-VG@Cu, 15-N-CQDs-VG@Cu, 20-N-CQDs-VG@Cu, and 25-N-CQDs-VG@Cu have overpotentials of 576 mV, 582 mV, 593 mV, and 638 mV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7b, the Tafel slope of different concentrations of CQDs loaded on graphene is compared. 5-N-CQDs-VG@Cu exhibits the best reaction kinetics in the HER process. Tafel slope increases with increasing concentration of CQDs. The HER stabilities of N-CQDs-VG@Cu with different concentrations of CQDs in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 is shown in Fig. 7c. The electrocatalysts are prepared with different concentrations of CQDs, which show good stability at 10 mA cm−2 within 10 h. And the Cu sheet shows good stability in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 at 10 mA cm−2 within 10 h, which indicates that the Cu substrate in N-CQDs-VG@Cu electrocatalyst has the same stability in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4.

As shown in Fig. 8 a, the overpotential of Pt/C@Cu is 86 mV at 10 mA cm−2. N-CQDs-VG@Cu has the lowest overpotential of H-CQDs-VG@Cu catalysts in the HER process, which is 496 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, and the overpotential in the HER process of S-CQDs-VG@Cu (513 mV) is higher than N-CQDs-VG@Cu. P-CQDs-VG@Cu has the highest overpotential of heteroatom-doped electrocatalysts. At 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential of CQDs-VG@Cu, and VG@Cu are 732 mV, and 745 mV, respectively. The Tafel slope of H-CQDs-VG@Cu is shown in Fig. 8b, the Tafel slope of Pt/C@Cu is 98.55 mV·dec−1. The N-CQDs-VG@Cu has the lowest Tafel slope value of other H-CQDs-VG@Cu catalysts is 117.75 mV·dec−1, and the Tafel slope of other catalysts increase sequentially as S-CQDs-VG@Cu (132.00 mV·dec−1), B-CQDs-VG@Cu (141.12 mV·dec−1), P-CQDs-VG@Cu (151.19 mV·dec−1), CQDs-VG@Cu (246.76 mV·dec−1), and VG@Cu (263.81 mV·dec−1). The N-CQDs-VG@Cu has a similar Tafel slope compared to commercial Pt/C, which indicates a better potential of N-CQDs-VG@Cu as an efficient electrocatalyst. Electrochemical property shows that N-CQDs-VG@Cu has the lowest overpotential and the smallest Tafel slope, indicating that N-CQDs-VG@Cu has the highest catalytic activity of H-CQDs-VG@Cu in the HER process [58,59]. And VG loading CQDs can effectively improve the electrocatalytic performance in the HER process, which is consistent with the results of previous DFT calculations. The stabilities of H-CQDs-VG@Cu is shown in Fig. 8c. H-CQDs-VG@Cu maintains good stability during long-term test (over more than 10 h). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurement is shown in Fig. 8d, Rs, CPE, and Rct in Fig. 8d represents the solution resistance, constant direction element, and charge transfer resistance, respectively. N-CQDs-VG@Cu shows the lowest resistance in H-CDQs-VG@Cu. The other impedances of H-CQDs-VG@Cu electrocatalysts from small to large are S-CQDs-VG@Cu, B-CQDs-VG@Cu, and P-CQDs-VG@Cu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, and XPS analysis of H-CQDs-VG@Cu after HER
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD result is shown in Fig. 9 a. The result of XRD shows that the diffraction peaks at 26.2° correspond to the graphitic carbon (PDF#41–1487). And the structure after the HER process is similar to that before the HER process, which indicates the good stability of N-CQDs-VG@Cu electrocatalyst in the HER process. The results of XPS measurement of N-CQDs-VG@Cu is shown in Fig. 9b, and c, the C 1s spectrum shows three peaks, which is C=C (284.8 eV) [60], C–N (285.2 eV) and C=O (286.6 eV) [61], respectively. The N 1s spectrum shows three peaks, which is pyridinic N (398.4 eV), pyrrolic N (399.3 eV), and graphitic N (400.9 eV) [62]. As shown in Fig. 9d, pyridinic N is the most abundant doping form of all N. The content of pyridinic N is reduced from 2.4604% to 1.5465%, which is due to that as the main catalytically active site in the HER process [63]. The results of the XPS analysis are consistent with the results of the simulated computational analysis, which further illustrates the feasibility of the H-CQDs-VG@Cu electrocatalyst constructed in the simulated computational part. The content of N atoms changes less after the HER process, which indicates that the VG shows better stability during the HER process [64].

Heteroatom doped has two doping forms in CQDs, H–2C, and H–3C. A comparison of DFT calculations and electrochemical measurements shows that P-CQDs-VG@Cu has the worst catalytic activity in H-CQDs-VG@Cu and P–2C also has the worst catalytic activity in H–2C, but P–3C has the best catalytic activity in H–3C, which indicates that H–2C is the main doping form in the catalyst. We speculate that CQDs as a ‘zero’ dimension material [46] has more edge-positioned C atoms substituted in heteroatom doping, which is the cause of the H–2C being the main doping form. Further, the catalytic performance of the electrocatalyst depends mainly on the form of doping that has high catalytic activity in H–2C. The theoretical models corresponding to N-CQDs-VG@Cu (117.75 mV·dec−1), S-CQDs-VG@Cu (132.00 mV·dec−1), B-CQDs-VG@Cu (141.12 mV·dec−1) and P-CQDs-VG@Cu (151.19 mV·dec−1) are N-CQDs-VG (0.2066 eV), S–FHC–CQDs-VG (0.2281 eV), B-CQDs-VG (0.4312 eV) and P-CQDs-VG (1.1259 eV), respectively.

In summary, we have explored the HER process occurring on H-CQDs-VG s by constructing a theoretical model and the DFT calculations. The synergy effect between VG and CQDs and the most suitable doping form for different heteroatoms is revealed by the theoretical calculations. N-doped shows the best electrocatalytic activity in DFT calculations and electrochemical measurements, which is the most promising doping element. This study helps to promote the rational design and development of carbon based electrocatalysts for water splitting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key Technology Research and Development Program of Shaanxi province (CN) (No. 2021KW-24) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21902127).
